<!-- Generated by Embody/Envoy - Do not remove this comment -->

# Visual Aesthetics

Make rendered image and motion output read clearly, intentionally, and with hierarchy. This skill is about what appears on screen, not node or network layout.

## When to Load This Skill

Load this skill before building or refining any network whose purpose is visual output: generative art, VJ visuals, shaders, render scenes, projection content, UI visuals, or captured TOP output. Pair it with `capture_top` to evaluate the actual result against the rubric at the end.

## Core Rule

Every frame needs hierarchy. Decide what the viewer should notice first, second, and last, then use value, color, scale, focus, texture, motion, and framing to make that order unavoidable.

## Preview and Judge (mandatory)

- After wiring any renderable chain, use `capture_top` on the OUTPUT TOP and actually look at the result. Judge it against the user's request AND the self-check rubric at the end of this skill. NEVER declare a visual task done without capturing and assessing the frame -- a clean network is not success; a good-looking, correct frame is.
- If the captured frame is black or empty, diagnose before continuing: (a) display/render flags on the output op; (b) for 3D, that a camera, a light, and geometry with render/display on all exist; (c) the chain terminates in a cooking Null; (d) no bypass flag is on; (e) resolution is not 0; (f) alpha/premultiply if the image seems present but invisible.
- Iterate: adjust the smallest set of upstream controls, re-capture, re-judge. This is the build -> verify -> preview loop.

## Composition and Framing

| Objective principle | How to achieve it in TD | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Use one clear focal point. A frame reads as "good" when the eye knows where to land first. | Choose the subject before building effects. Make it the place with the strongest contrast, sharpest detail, most distinct color, largest shape, or unique motion. Use camera placement, Transform/Crop style composition tools, or layered Composite/Over workflows to support it. Verify exact TOP and parameter names against the TD wiki. | Multiple evenly intense elements compete, so the image feels busy even if each element is attractive. |
| Place the subject deliberately. Centered symmetry feels stable and iconic; off-center placement feels more dynamic. | Use rule-of-thirds placement as a default: put the subject near a 1/3 or 2/3 position unless a centered, symmetrical read is intentional. Balance asymmetry with visual weight from size, brightness, saturation, density, or motion. | The subject drifts near the center by accident, or a heavy bright object pulls attention away from the intended subject. |
| Preserve negative space. Breathing room helps the viewer separate subject from field. | Leave quieter areas with lower detail, lower contrast, lower saturation, or slower motion. Use Crop/Transform or camera framing to avoid filling every pixel. | Every area has equal density, making the output read as wallpaper or noise rather than composition. |
| Use depth cues. Overlap, scale, focus, atmosphere, and contrast gradients make flat images feel spatial. | Layer elements with Composite/Over techniques. Create foreground, midground, and background roles. Consider blur-by-distance, fog-like desaturation, lower contrast in distant layers, or scale changes. Confirm the specific TD operator choices and parameter names against the wiki. | All elements sit on the same visual plane, so the render feels flat even if it has many layers. |
| Avoid tangents and awkward crops. Edges and intersections strongly affect perceived polish. | Check the captured frame for objects barely touching borders, lines grazing corners, or shapes kissing each other. Reframe with the camera, crop, transform, or layer offsets. In TD render coordinates, bottom-left is (0, 0) and Y increases upward; account for this when sampling, cropping, or writing pixels. | Important shapes are clipped at joints, touch the frame edge unintentionally, or align in ways that look accidental. |

## Value

| Objective principle | How to achieve it in TD | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Value structure reads before color. If the lightness pattern works, the image remains legible even without hue. | Create distinct dark, mid, and light areas. Capture the output and inspect a luminance or desaturated view. Use the squint test: the frame should still read as clear shapes. | The render depends on color differences only; when desaturated, the subject disappears. |
| Use a real value range without accidental clipping. Full range gives energy; controlled limits preserve detail. | Use a Level TOP, curve, LUT, or equivalent grading stage to set black point, white point, and mid contrast. Verify exact operator and parameter names against the TD wiki. Keep pure black or pure white only when intentional. | Muddy mid-tone-only output looks weak; clipped output loses form and washes out highlights or shadows. |
| Put the highest local value contrast on the subject. The eye goes where adjacent light and dark differ most. | Reserve the sharpest dark-light transition for the focal point. Lower contrast in backgrounds or supporting layers with grading, fog-like treatment, blur, or lower opacity. | Background edges have more contrast than the subject, so attention goes to the wrong place. |
| Check value separately from effects. Glow, feedback, and compositing can collapse structure. | After adding post effects, re-check the luminance view or histogram if available. Treat histogram/operator names as wiki-verified details, not assumptions. | Bloom, feedback, or opacity stacking turns clear values into a flat gray haze. |

## Color

| Objective principle | How to achieve it in TD | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Limit the palette. A controlled palette reads designed because related hues reduce cognitive noise. | Pick a harmony before adding layers: monochromatic, analogous, complementary, split-complementary, triadic, or dominant plus accent. Use gradient ramps, LUTs, Lookup/Level/HSV-to-RGB style color operators, or other color-grade techniques to map many sources into one palette. Verify exact TD operator names and parameters against the wiki. | Many unrelated hues compete, making the image look accidental or generated by defaults. |
| Use dominant plus accent. One color family should carry most of the frame; the accent should mark importance. | Keep most layers muted or within one hue range. Put a small, higher-saturation accent on or near the focal point. | Every object is equally saturated, so nothing feels intentional and the eye has no priority. |
| Control temperature. Warm/cool contrast creates depth and mood when one temperature dominates. | Decide whether the frame is mostly warm or mostly cool. Use the opposite temperature sparingly for the subject, rim, background separation, or depth cue. | Warm and cool areas appear in equal random amounts, making color feel noisy instead of expressive. |
| Let color support value, not replace it. Hue cannot rescue a weak lightness structure. | Grade after value composition. Check desaturated output, then restore color and make sure hue reinforces the same focal hierarchy. | Bright colors fight the value plan, pulling attention away from the subject. |

## Contrast

| Objective principle | How to achieve it in TD | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Contrast is the engine of interest. It can come from value, hue, temperature, saturation, size, shape, texture, focus, sharpness, or motion. | Choose 1 to 3 contrast types to emphasize. For example: high value contrast on the subject, lower saturation in the field, and unique motion for the focal layer. | Every dimension is pushed at once, creating visual shouting instead of hierarchy. |
| Figure-ground separation is non-negotiable. The subject must separate from its background. | Separate subject and background through value, color temperature, edge sharpness, focus, scale, opacity, or motion. If the subject is bright, keep the immediate background darker or cooler; if it is soft, keep the background simpler. | The subject and background share the same value, color, sharpness, and motion, so the subject disappears. |
| Avoid uniformity. Equal contrast everywhere reads as flat or noisy. | Reduce contrast in secondary layers. Let some regions be calm, soft, dark, low saturation, or slow. | Every pixel area has equal detail, size, sharpness, and motion energy. |

## Motion and Time

| Objective principle | How to achieve it in TD | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Prefer eased motion over pure linear motion. Natural motion accelerates and decelerates. | Use eased or curved interpolation for camera movement, fades, transforms, and parameter animation. Confirm exact CHOP, DAT, expression, or keyframe tools against the TD wiki before naming parameters. | Everything moves with constant speed, which reads robotic unless mechanical precision is the intent. |
| Add variation and secondary motion. Small timing differences create life and scale. | Use noise, LFO-style modulation, phase offsets, delay, or per-element variation. De-sync repeated elements slightly instead of driving all copies from the exact same timing. Verify operator choices against the wiki. | All elements start, stop, pulse, rotate, or flash at the same time, making the scene feel procedural in the wrong way. |
| Use motion contrast as a focal tool. The eye goes to the thing moving differently from its surroundings. | Keep background motion slower, smoother, or lower amplitude. Let the subject have distinct timing, direction, speed, or stillness. Stillness can be the contrast if everything else moves. | Global motion affects every layer equally, so motion adds energy but no focus. |
| Build rhythm with rest. Constant intensity exhausts attention. | Alternate active passages with quieter moments. Use pauses, slower cycles, lower amplitude, or temporary simplification. Avoid harsh pops unless they are deliberate cuts or hits. | The output is frenetic at all times, or abrupt jumps happen without rhythmic purpose. |

## Finishing and Post

| Objective principle | How to achieve it in TD | Failure mode |
|---|---|---|
| Post effects should focus the image, not advertise themselves. | Add a final grade after the main composite. Use glow/bloom on highlights, subtle vignette, gentle grain or dither, and tiny chromatic aberration or other lens imperfections only when they support the subject. Verify exact TOP/operator names and parameters against the TD wiki. | Every effect is at high strength, so the finish looks like a preset stack. |
| Preserve value structure through bloom and glow. Highlights can bloom without destroying form. | Keep glow strength low enough that edges and subject silhouette remain readable. Re-check the luminance view after adding glow. | Bloom washes the frame into a pale haze and removes the dark-mid-light structure. |
| Prevent banding in gradients. Smooth gradients often need texture. | Add subtle dither, grain, or low-strength noise near the end of the chain when gradients show steps. | Clean mathematical gradients band visibly, especially after compression or projection. |
| Use restraint. Small corrections accumulate into polish. | Treat finishing as a sequence of low-strength adjustments. Toggle each post stage on and off and keep it only if it improves focus, depth, or value clarity. | Post processing becomes the subject instead of supporting the subject. |

## Objective Self-Check Rubric

Use `capture_top` to grab the render, then answer these before calling it done:

| Check | Pass condition |
|---|---|
| Focal point | There is one clear subject, and the viewer can name where the eye lands first. |
| Contrast location | The highest value, color, focus, size, texture, or motion contrast supports the subject. |
| Squint test | In a luminance or desaturated view, the image still reads as distinct shapes. |
| Value range | The frame has darks, mids, and lights without accidental pure-black or pure-white clipping. |
| Palette | Hues are limited, saturation is controlled, and one temperature or color family dominates. |
| Figure-ground | The subject separates from its background by value, color, focus, scale, or motion. |
| Composition | The subject placement, crop, negative space, and depth cues look intentional. |
| Motion | Animation is eased, has variation, contains focal motion or focal stillness, and includes moments of rest. |
| Finishing | Glow, vignette, grain, aberration, and grade are restrained and improve clarity. |

If a render fails a check, adjust the smallest number of upstream controls needed to restore hierarchy.
